name: GitHub Issues AI Triage → Notion Board

parameters.content:
## GitHub Issues AI Triage → Notion Board

When a new issue is opened on a GitHub repository, this workflow uses an AI model to classify it by type (bug, feature, question, documentation, other), assign a priority level (high, medium, low), and generate a one-sentence summary. Results are written to a Notion database and posted as a comment on the GitHub issue.

No external triage service. No SaaS subscription. Runs on your own n8n instance with your own Notion database.

### How it works

1. **GitHub Webhook Trigger** — Listens for the "issues" event with action "opened" on the configured repository. Fires only when a new issue is created. Does not trigger on comments, edits, or closes.

2. **Extract Issue Data** — Parses the webhook payload. GitHub Trigger wraps the full payload as a JSON string in the "body" field, so this node extracts the actual issue object, then pulls out the fields the workflow needs: issue number, title, body text (truncated to 2000 chars to save LLM tokens), URL, author, labels, and repository full name. Falls back to parsing the repository_url field if the repository object is missing from the payload.

3. **Duplicate Check (Notion)** — Queries the Notion database for an existing entry with the same Issue URL. If a match is found, the workflow stops — this issue was already triaged. If the Notion API fails (timeout, rate limit, invalid token), the error branch fires and the workflow continues anyway. A failed duplicate check should not block triage.

4. **AI Triage (Qwen 3 via OpenRouter)** — Sends the issue title and body to the model with a structured prompt that asks for: category, priority, summary, and suggested labels. The prompt includes clear definitions for each category and priority level so the model classifies consistently. If the LLM call fails, a fallback node assigns default values (category: other, priority: medium) so the issue is still tracked in Notion.

5. **Create Notion Entry** — Writes a new row to the Notion database with all fields populated: title, issue URL, author, category, priority, summary, suggested labels, status (Open), source (ai or fallback), and creation date.

6. **Post GitHub Comment** — Adds a formatted comment on the GitHub issue showing the triage result: category, priority, summary, and suggested labels. If the triage used fallback values (LLM was unavailable), a warning is included in the comment.

### Why this approach

Most issue triage tools push results back into GitHub as labels or comments. This workflow pushes to Notion instead. The reason: teams already use Notion as their project management hub. Having issues auto-classified and waiting in a Notion database means you can filter, sort, and assign without switching tools.

The AI model does classification, not decision-making. It doesn't close issues or assign people — it categorizes and summarizes. A human reviews the Notion board and decides what to act on. This keeps the human in the loop while removing the manual sorting work.

The duplicate check prevents wasted API calls. If an issue was already triaged (maybe someone triggered the webhook twice, or re-opened a closed issue), the workflow skips it instead of creating a duplicate Notion entry.

Self-hosted means your issue data never leaves your infrastructure. The only external call is to the LLM provider (OpenRouter by default), and you can replace that with a self-hosted model to keep everything internal.

### Setup (10–15 min)

- [ ] Create a GitHub Fine-grained Personal Access Token: Settings → Developer settings → Fine-grained tokens. Permissions: Issues (read/write), Webhooks (read/write). Scope it to the repository you want to watch.
- [ ] In n8n, add the GitHub credential using the token.
- [ ] Create a Notion database with these properties:
  - Title (title)
  - Issue URL (url)
  - Author (rich_text)
  - Category (select: bug / feature / question / documentation / other)
  - Priority (select: high / medium / low)
  - Summary (rich_text)
  - Suggested Labels (rich_text)
  - Status (select: Open / Closed)
  - Source (select: ai / fallback)
  - Created (date)
- [ ] Create a Notion internal integration at https://www.notion.so/my-integrations. Copy the token.
- [ ] In n8n, add the Notion credential using the integration token.
- [ ] Share the Notion database with the integration (database → ... → Connections → select integration).
- [ ] In the "When Issue Opened" node: set Owner and Repository to match your GitHub repo.
- [ ] In the "Verify Notion Duplicate" and "Add to Notion Board" nodes: set the Database to your Notion database.
- [ ] Add your OpenRouter API key to the "OpenAI Qwen-3 Model" node credential.
- [ ] Activate the workflow. Open a test issue on your GitHub repo.

### Customization

- **Swap the LLM**: Change the model in "OpenAI Qwen-3 Model" to any OpenRouter-supported model (GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini). Or point to a self-hosted model by changing the base URL in the credential.
- **Adjust triage rules**: Edit the prompt in "Initiate AI Triage" to add custom categories specific to your project (e.g., "performance", "accessibility", "security").
- **Multi-repo**: Duplicate the workflow for each repo you want to triage, or modify the trigger to use a GitHub App that watches multiple repos.
- **Add Slack or Discord notification**: After "Post GitHub Comment", add a node to send a summary to your team chat.
- **Auto-assign**: Add logic after "Format AI Triage Results" to assign GitHub issues based on category (e.g., bugs to backend team, docs to content team).

### What's next

This is a foundation. Here's where it can grow:

- **Auto-label on GitHub** — Use the suggested labels from AI to automatically apply labels on the issue. Requires label creation in the repo first.
- **Batch triage** — Add a "Triage All Open Issues" mode that processes existing untriaged issues, not just new ones.
- **Priority-based routing** — High priority issues get a Slack or Discord notification immediately. Medium and low go to Notion only.
- **Learning from feedback** — Track when a human changes the AI-assigned category in Notion, and use that as feedback to improve the prompt over time.
- **Notion dashboard** — Build a Notion view with filters by category and priority. Add a "Triage backlog" view sorted by priority.

Simple means it runs cheap, breaks rarely, and is easy to debug. Complexity can be added when the use case demands it.

### Requirements

- n8n instance (self-hosted or cloud)
- GitHub Fine-grained Personal Access Token (Issues read/write, Webhooks read/write)
- Notion account with internal integration
- OpenRouter API key (or any OpenAI-compatible endpoint)

## GitHub Issue Trigger

**When Issue Opened** — GitHub Trigger node that registers a webhook on the configured repository. Listens only for the "issues" event. When someone opens a new issue, GitHub sends a POST request to the n8n webhook endpoint with the full issue payload.

The trigger does not fire on issue edits, comments, label changes, or closes. Only new issues enter this workflow.

Credential needed: GitHub API (Fine-grained PAT with Issues read/write and Webhooks read/write permissions). Set the Owner and Repository fields to match the repo you want to watch.

## Extract and Check Issues

Four nodes that parse the incoming issue and decide whether it needs triage.

**Extract GitHub Issue Data** — The GitHub Trigger wraps the webhook payload as a JSON string inside the "body" field. This node parses that string, extracts the issue object, then pulls out the fields the workflow needs: issue number, title, body (truncated to 2000 characters to stay within LLM token limits), URL, author, labels, and repository full name. Falls back to parsing the repository_url field if the repository object is missing from the payload.

**Verify Notion Duplicate** — Queries the Notion database using the Issue URL as a filter. If a matching page exists, the issue was already triaged and the workflow stops. "Always Output Data" is enabled so the node always produces output even when no match is found. "onError" is set to "continueErrorOutput" so a Notion API failure does not block triage — the error branch sends the issue forward anyway.

**Identify New Issue** — Code node that checks the Notion query result. Three cases: (1) Notion returned an error object → issue passes through with duplicate_check "error"; (2) Notion returned an empty result (no match) → issue passes through with duplicate_check "passed"; (3) Notion found a matching page with an "id" field → returns empty array, workflow stops. This issue was already triaged — no duplicate entry created.

**Bypass Duplicate Check** — Error branch from Verify Notion Duplicate. If the Notion API call fails (timeout, rate limit, invalid token), this node passes the issue forward with duplicate_check "skipped". The workflow continues to AI triage regardless of the Notion check failure. Better to over-triage than to miss an issue because of a transient API error.

## AI Triage Processing

Four nodes that handle the LLM call, its output, and error fallback.

**Initiate AI Triage** — Chain LLM node that sends the issue title and body to the model with a structured prompt. The prompt defines five categories (bug, feature, question, documentation, other), three priority levels (high, medium, low), and asks for a one-sentence summary plus 1-3 suggested labels. Each level has clear definitions so the model classifies consistently. "onError" is set to "continueErrorOutput" — if the LLM call fails, the error branch catches it instead of crashing the workflow.

**OpenAI Qwen-3 Model** — Sub-node providing the language model. Default is Qwen 3 235B via OpenRouter. Can be swapped for any OpenRouter-supported model (GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini) or a self-hosted model by changing the credential and model name.

**Format AI Triage Results** — Parses the LLM output. Strips Qwen 3 thinking tags, extracts the JSON block from the response, validates each field against allowed values (category must be one of five options, priority must be one of three), and caps the summary at 200 characters. If parsing fails entirely, defaults to category "other", priority "medium". This node never throws — it always returns a valid triage object.

**Handle AI Triage Error** — Error branch from Initiate AI Triage. If the LLM call fails (API timeout, rate limit, model unavailable), this node creates a fallback triage result: category "other", priority "medium", summary "AI triage failed — manual review needed", source "fallback". The issue still gets a Notion entry so it is never silently dropped.

## Notion Entry and GitHub Comment

Three nodes that write the triage result to Notion and notify on GitHub.

**Add to Notion Board** — Creates a new page in the Notion database with all triage fields: title, issue URL, author, category, priority, summary, suggested labels, status (defaults to "Open"), source (ai or fallback), and creation date. Both the success branch (Format AI Triage Results) and the error branch (Handle AI Triage Error) converge here — every issue that passes the duplicate check gets a Notion entry, whether triaged by AI or assigned fallback values.

**Draft GitHub Comment** — Builds the comment body from the Notion entry. Reads triage fields from the Notion output (property_category, property_priority, property_summary, property_suggested_labels, property_source) and formats them into a readable comment with markdown. Extracts the repository owner and name from the issue URL by parsing the GitHub URL structure. If the source is "fallback", appends a warning that AI triage was unavailable.

**Post GitHub Comment** — Posts the formatted comment on the GitHub issue using the Issues API. The comment shows category, priority, summary, and suggested labels so anyone visiting the issue can see the triage result at a glance. Owner and repository values are passed dynamically — this workflow does not hardcode any repository names.

parameters.jsCode:
const issueData = $('Extract GitHub Issue Data').first().json;
return [{ json: { ...issueData, duplicate_check: 'skipped' } }];

parameters.text:
=You are a GitHub issue triage assistant. Analyze the issue below and respond with a valid JSON object only. No explanation, no markdown, just JSON.

{
  "category": "bug" | "feature" | "question" | "documentation" | "other",
  "priority": "high" | "medium" | "low",
  "summary": "one-sentence plain English summary",
  "suggested_labels": ["label1", "label2"]
}

Rules:
- category "bug": something is broken, errors, unexpected behavior
- category "feature": request for new functionality or improvement
- category "question": asking how to do something, setup help
- category "documentation": docs missing, unclear, or outdated
- category "other": anything that doesn't fit above
- priority "high": security issue, data loss, complete blocker, affects all users
- priority "medium": significant but not critical, workaround exists
- priority "low": nice to have, minor inconvenience, cosmetic
- summary must be under 100 characters
- suggested_labels: 1-3 short labels that describe the issue (lowercase, hyphenated)

Issue title: {{ $json.title }}
Issue body: {{ $json.body }}
Repository: {{ $json.repo_full_name }}

JSON:
